open_items: Ratify or replace the candidate PSM1 format before stable boot ABI promotion.
Define and implement signed manifest authentication, trust anchors, algorithm agility, and revocation policy.
Persist and atomically enforce the minimum secure version across update and rollback.
Define the final transfer-time CR3 activation, stack switch, register ABI, and explicit revocation plan.
Authenticate the loaded initial-system, recovery, policy, symbols, microcode, and firmware artifacts through a ratified trust policy.
Implement PINIT1 parsing and semantic enforcement in PooleBoot before initial-system declarations can cross the firmware boundary.
Implement PooleKernel activation, capability issuance, transactional startup, rollback, and lifecycle enforcement for qualified PINIT1 declarations.
Implement authenticated PREC1 state persistence, PooleBoot enforcement, handoff binding, and PooleKernel-mediated recovery without ambient authority.
Implement signed PSYM1 parsing and bounded capability-authorized diagnostic consumption in PooleBoot and PooleKernel without staging private debug data.

Authenticate PMCU1, validate real vendor containers and licenses, observe privileged per-processor revisions, and implement early PooleKernel apply and post-verify enforcement before any production payload is included or applied.

Implement live bounded FMP, ESRT, capsule, and exact device-plugin inventory adapters, vendor package validation, recovery evidence, and separately authorized update execution before PFWM1 can move beyond synthetic dry-run qualification.

Implement signed PPOL1 verification and live PooleBoot/PooleKernel intersection enforcement, mode transitions, capability issuance, revocation, rollback, and durable audit before policy decisions can be applied.
Transfer to PooleKernel and capture entry, panic, recovery, and reset evidence.
Add fault-injected live EFI_INVALID_PARAMETER retry evidence rather than lifecycle-model evidence alone.
Define how PooleKernel reclaims final-map scratch pools after consuming PBP1.
Qualify hostile firmware behavior, target firmware, physical hardware, and a second clean builder.
Complete Secure Boot, measured boot, TPM policy, signing, ISO, installer, and recovery gates.
claim_boundary: This contract proves bounded live UEFI filesystem discovery and exact PBC1, PSM1, and PKELF1 reads from deterministic development media.
PBC1 selects SYSTEM_A.PBM; PSM1 selects and digest-binds the kernel path, slot, version floor, exact file bytes, image bytes, and entry contract.
PKELF1 segments, BSS, and relative relocations are materialized in firmware pages and checked against an independent Python oracle.
Six PBART1 development envelopes are independently role-, version-, payload-digest-, whole-file-digest-, path-, size-, and PSM1-bound, then copied into distinct zero-padded loader page ranges.

The INITIAL.PBA payload is a deterministic PINIT1 declaration bundle validated by an independent host oracle; unsigned development activation is denied, while PooleBoot still treats the payload as opaque and PooleKernel does not activate it.

The RECOVERY.PBA payload is a deterministic PREC1 policy bundle validated by an independent host oracle; unsigned development activation is denied, while mutable state persistence, PooleBoot enforcement, PooleKernel authority mediation, and recovery execution remain absent.

The SYMBOLS.PBA payload is a deterministic PSYM1 public diagnostic index validated by an independent host oracle; unsigned consumption is denied, private split-debug data and runtime addresses stay off media, and no export or capability authority is created.

The MICROCOD.PBA payload is a deterministic PMCU1 package containing only synthetic never-apply test bytes; exact CPU selection and reset-based known-good policy are independently modeled, while unsigned activation is denied and no vendor validation, privileged revision probe, CPU update, or authority exists.

The FIRMWARE.PBA payload is a deterministic PFWM1 synthetic manifest validated by an independent host oracle; exact identities, version floors, dependencies, dry-run prerequisites, and post-reset receipts are modeled, while unsigned activation is denied and no payload bytes, live inventory, updater driver, capsule submission, firmware mutation, or authority exists.

The POLICY.PBA payload is a deterministic PPOL1 qualification-only bundle validated by independent Python and allocation-free Rust oracles; six default-deny mode ceilings and all 11 PINIT1 capability routes are cross-bound, while unsigned activation, live enforcement, state mutation, authority creation, and PooleGlyph executable authority remain absent.
PKMAP2 expands the r, rx, r, and rw kernel plan into exact supervisor 4 KiB leaves plus an eight-page guarded NX stack and a 256-page read-only NX handoff range.
PooleBoot activates the candidate CR3 only for complete translation, permission, alias-hash, guard, and framebuffer checks, with zero firmware calls, then restores the original CR3.
The kernel, six PBART1 ranges, four private page-table pages, stack pages, and handoff pages remain retained through successful ExitBootServices.
PBLIVE3 constructs PBP1 from the final descriptor-stride-aware map and cross-binds every retained range as loader-reserved memory.
The final PBP1 records boot-services-exited state, stack top, candidate root, handoff addresses, exact artifact roles 1 through 7 and digests, PBC1 policy, PSM1 selection, and GOP state.
PBEXIT1 permits at most four attempts, retries only EFI_INVALID_PARAMETER with a fresh map, and never returns to UEFI after the first attempt begins.
After success, PooleBoot performs no firmware calls, emits direct-port evidence, disables interrupts, and halts forever before transfer.
The exited PBP1 is still non-transferable because all artifacts remain unsigned, required entropy and firmware/CPU records are absent, and no artifact semantics or kernel-entry transfer occurs.
Framebuffer translation and cache equality prove no drift from firmware state, not a qualified final PooleOS cache policy.
Digest equality is not manifest authentication: no signature, trust anchor, persistent rollback state, Secure Boot enforcement, measured boot, TPM use, owner signature, or trust promotion is claimed.
QEMU/OVMF evidence is a single-host non-promoting development result, not target-firmware or physical-hardware qualification.
This does not satisfy the N5 exit gate, produce a bootable ISO, authorize signing or publication, or establish production readiness.
